problems.title: Sudan famine & displacement crisis
problems.blurb: Sudan's civil war has produced the world's largest displacement and hunger emergency, with confirmed famine and a 2026 UN response plan funded at under 16% of the ~$9bn needed.
problems.discoveredFrom: UN/OCHA Global Humanitarian Overview 2026 and Stimson Center both flag Sudan as the largest yet most under-funded and media-marginalised crisis globally.

problems.title: DR Congo Ebola outbreak (2026)
problems.blurb: An expanding Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo had confirmed 1,406 cases and 438 deaths by July 2026, straining treatment capacity even though proven vaccines and monoclonal-antibody therapies exist to contain it.
problems.discoveredFrom: WHO July 2026 emergency briefings and a 6 July 2026 UNICEF flash update report ~38 new confirmed Ebola cases/day in the DRC with treatment beds ~96% occupied.

clean-water.summary: As of the 2025 WHO/UNICEF Joint Monitoring Programme update, 2.1 billion people — 1 in 4 globally — still lack safely managed drinking water, including 106 million who drink untreated surface water. Progress is real but slow: 961 million people gained safely managed services between 2015 and 2024, lifting global coverage from 68% to 74%, with rural coverage rising from 50% to 60%. The gap is concentrated in low-income countries, fragile/conflict settings, and rural, indigenous and minority communities. The strongest evidence base is for point-of-use water treatment: a meta-analysis of 18 RCTs found chlorination cut all-cause under-5 mortality by roughly 24%, at about USD 40 per DALY averted — on par with the most cost-effective child-health interventions like vaccination.

interventions.title: Point-of-use and in-line chlorination
interventions.evidence: A University of Chicago meta-analysis of 18 RCTs (NBER w30835) found water treatment, driven by chlorination, reduced all-cause child mortality ~24% at roughly USD 40 per DALY averted.
interventions.actor: Health ministries, water utilities and NGOs (e.g. Evidence Action's Dispensers for Safe Water)

interventions.title: Chlorine dispensers at collection points
interventions.evidence: The same meta-analysis estimated USD 28–72 per DALY averted for point-of-collection dispensers and free chlorine programs, ~45x below the 1x-GDP cost-effectiveness threshold.
interventions.actor: Local governments and community water committees

interventions.title: Piped, safely managed supply expansion
interventions.evidence: WHO/UNICEF JMP 2025 credits the 68%→74% coverage rise partly to expanded managed piped systems, the durable route out of surface-water reliance.
interventions.actor: National water utilities with World Bank / development-bank finance

interventions.title: WASH investment in fragile and rural contexts
interventions.evidence: WHO/UNICEF 2025 identify low-income, fragile, rural and indigenous populations as facing the widest disparities, requiring targeted funding.
interventions.actor: Donors, UN agencies and national governments

malaria-tb-hiv.summary: These three treatable diseases still kill well over 2 million people a year, concentrated in low-income countries. Malaria caused an estimated 610,000 deaths and 282 million cases in 2024 (about 9 million more cases than the prior year), with ~95% of deaths in the WHO African Region and 11 countries carrying two-thirds of the burden. Tuberculosis remains the leading single-agent infectious killer, with 1.23 million deaths in 2024 (including 150,000 among people with HIV) and a record 8.2 million people newly diagnosed. People living with HIV are about 12 times more likely to develop TB, making integrated HIV/TB care essential. Proven tools exist and are scaling — since 2000 an estimated 2.3 billion malaria cases and 14 million deaths have been averted — but artemisinin partial resistance is now confirmed or suspected in at least 8 African countries, threatening the gains.

interventions.title: Insecticide-treated bed nets (ITNs)
interventions.evidence: A 2024 cost-effectiveness review (1Day Sooner / MMV) found ITNs the most economical malaria-prevention method in Africa, narrowly ahead of the R21 vaccine.
interventions.actor: The Global Fund, PMI and national malaria programs

interventions.title: R21/Matrix-M and RTS,S malaria vaccines
interventions.evidence: Both cut malaria cases >50% in year one; R21 costs ~USD 3.90/dose vs ~USD 10.90 for RTS,S with comparable efficacy, per WHO and Serum Institute data.
interventions.actor: Gavi, WHO and health ministries in endemic countries

interventions.title: Seasonal malaria chemoprevention combined with vaccination
interventions.evidence: A Lancet Infectious Diseases phase-3 trial (Burkina Faso/Mali) showed vaccine plus SMC cut clinical malaria, severe disease and deaths ~75% versus either alone.
interventions.actor: National programs with Global Fund / Unitaid support

interventions.title: TB case-finding, treatment and preventive therapy
interventions.evidence: WHO's TB fact sheet reports a record 8.2 million people diagnosed in 2023–24; scaling diagnosis and treatment is the main lever against the 1.23 million annual deaths.
interventions.actor: WHO End TB program and national TB programs

interventions.title: Integrated HIV/TB care and antiretroviral therapy
interventions.evidence: WHO notes people with HIV are ~12x more likely to develop TB and TB is their leading cause of death; co-management and ART sharply cut mortality.
interventions.actor: PEPFAR, The Global Fund and national HIV programs

air-pollution.summary: Air pollution is one of the largest environmental health risks, associated with about 7 million premature deaths a year from combined ambient and household exposure; a UNICEF/HEI State of Global Air analysis attributed 8.1 million deaths to air pollution in 2021, making it the second-leading risk factor for death, including for children under five. More than 90% of these deaths trace to fine particulate matter (PM2.5), which penetrates the lungs and bloodstream; roughly 68% of outdoor-pollution deaths are from ischaemic heart disease and stroke. The strongest recent evidence is that household fuel transitions drive large exposure and mortality reductions: switching from biomass to LPG in India cut kitchen PM2.5 ~93% and personal exposure ~78%, and China's shift away from household solid fuels was the dominant factor in its 2005–2015 PM2.5 mortality decline, with clean-fuel switching estimated to avert on the order of half a million deaths a year.

interventions.title: Household clean-cooking (LPG) transition
interventions.evidence: The HAPIN trial in India recorded a sustained ~93% drop in kitchen PM2.5 and ~78% drop in personal exposure among women switching from biomass to LPG.
interventions.actor: National energy ministries (e.g. India's PMUY) with WHO and clean-cooking funds

interventions.title: Phasing out household and small-scale coal
interventions.evidence: A PNAS study found household-fuel change dominated China's 2005–2015 PM2.5 exposure and mortality decline; clean-fuel switching could avert ~500,000 deaths/year.
interventions.actor: City and provincial governments (e.g. Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei coal bans)

interventions.title: Tighter ambient air-quality standards toward WHO guidelines
interventions.evidence: WHO estimates meeting interim target 1 (35 µg/m³ PM2.5) would save ~300,000 lives a year worldwide.
interventions.actor: National environment agencies enforcing WHO Air Quality Guidelines

interventions.title: Cleaner transport and industrial emission controls
interventions.evidence: WHO attributes ~68% of outdoor-pollution deaths to heart disease and stroke driven largely by traffic and industrial PM2.5, targetable via fuel and emission standards.
interventions.actor: Transport and industry regulators, city clean-air zones

maternal-child-health.summary: Most maternal and child deaths remain preventable with cheap, proven care, yet progress has stalled. In 2024 an estimated 4.9 million children died before their fifth birthday — roughly one death every six seconds — with neonatal deaths (first 28 days) accounting for 2.3 million, or 47%; more than four in five under-five deaths occurred in sub-Saharan Africa (2.8 million, 58%) and Southern Asia (1.2 million, 25%). About 260,000 women died in pregnancy and childbirth in 2023; the global maternal mortality ratio fell 40% since 2000 (328→197 per 100,000 live births), but sub-Saharan Africa and Southern Asia account for ~87% of maternal deaths and low-income countries face an MMR of 346 versus 10 in high-income countries. The pace of under-five mortality reduction has slowed by more than 60% since 2015, and if current trends hold more than 27 million children are projected to die between 2025 and 2030 — a trajectory reversible with known interventions that return up to USD 20 per USD 1 invested.

interventions.title: Skilled birth attendance and emergency obstetric care
interventions.evidence: WHO identifies postpartum haemorrhage, sepsis, hypertensive disorders and obstructed labour as the leading, largely avoidable causes of maternal death; skilled attendance plus basic emergency obstetric care (oxytocics, antibiotics, manual placenta removal) is the core response, with a target of 90% of births skilled-attended.
interventions.actor: Health ministries with WHO, UNFPA and midwifery programs

interventions.title: Oral rehydration salts (ORS) and zinc for childhood diarrhoea
interventions.evidence: GiveWell and UNICEF rate ORS plus zinc among the most cost-effective interventions for the leading diarrhoeal killer of young children, saving lives at very low cost when scaled.
interventions.actor: National child-health programs, Clinton Health Access Initiative and community health workers

interventions.title: Childhood vaccination and treatment of severe acute malnutrition
interventions.evidence: WHO/UNICEF March 2026 note vaccines, nutrition programmes and severe-malnutrition treatment deliver some of the highest returns in global health, with every USD 1 in child survival generating up to USD 20 in benefits.
interventions.actor: Gavi, WHO, UNICEF and national immunisation programs

interventions.title: Misoprostol / oxytocin for postpartum haemorrhage
interventions.evidence: Evidence reviewed by the Maternal Health Task Force finds misoprostol can prevent three of four postpartum-haemorrhage cases, and advance community-health-worker distribution is a safe, effective strategy where facility delivery is limited.
interventions.actor: National maternal-health programs and community health-worker networks

interventions.title: Antenatal care and family planning
interventions.evidence: A 2025 Lancet Global Health decomposition analysis attributes the 2000–2023 maternal-mortality decline to maternity-care improvement, fertility decline and expanded contraceptive use.
interventions.actor: Health ministries with UNFPA and reproductive-health NGOs

learning-poverty.summary: Learning poverty — the share of 10-year-olds who cannot read and understand a simple, age-appropriate text — stands at about 70% in low- and middle-income countries, up from 57% before the COVID-19 pandemic, a rise of roughly a third that the World Bank calls the worst shock to education in recorded history; the 70% figure was reaffirmed in an October 2025 World Bank literacy report. The problem combines children out of school with poor learning among those enrolled: an analysis of early-grade reading assessments covering more than 500,000 students in 48 countries and 96 languages found that after three years of schooling over 90% still could not reliably name letters, sound them out, or read simple words. The evidence base is unusually clear about what works — the Global Education Evidence Advisory Panel rates a small set of structured, targeted approaches as 'good buys' that raise learning cheaply and at scale.

interventions.title: Structured pedagogy
interventions.evidence: The Global Education Evidence Advisory Panel rates structured pedagogy — coherent packages of daily lesson plans, teacher guides, student materials and instructional coaching — as a 'good buy'; the World Bank's October 2025 report highlights it as the leading proven, cost-effective literacy model.
interventions.actor: Education ministries with the World Bank Foundational Learning Compact

interventions.title: Teaching at the Right Level (TaRL) / targeted instruction
interventions.evidence: Grouping children by learning level rather than grade is evidence-backed and has helped millions in South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa master foundational literacy and numeracy, per the What Works Hub for Global Education.
interventions.actor: Governments with Pratham and J-PAL

interventions.title: Structured early-grade and mother-tongue reading instruction
interventions.evidence: A 2024 review of structured-pedagogy programmes in sub-Saharan Africa (PMC11422479) documents consistent reading gains when programmes integrate core reading subskills and support teachers in early grades.
interventions.actor: Curriculum bodies and teacher-training institutions

interventions.title: Foundational-learning targets and measurement
interventions.evidence: UNICEF's 2025 Foundational Learning Action Tracker and the World Bank/UNESCO/UNICEF RAPID framework show that setting explicit targets and measuring reading drives national prioritisation and resource shifts.
interventions.actor: World Bank, UNICEF and UNESCO with national statistics offices

energy-access.summary: The 2025 Tracking SDG7 report (IEA, IRENA, UN Statistics Division, World Bank and WHO, June 2025) finds nearly 92% of the world now has basic electricity access, but more than 666 million people remain without it and the current pace will miss universal access by 2030. The gap has become sharply concentrated: 85% of people without electricity live in sub-Saharan Africa, up from 50% in 2010, even as Central and Southern Asia cut their deficit from 414 million in 2010 to just 27 million in 2023. Clean-cooking access rose from 64% in 2015 to 74% in 2023, yet about 2.1 billion people still rely on polluting fuels; in sub-Saharan Africa four in five families lack clean cooking and the number without it grows by roughly 14 million a year. Distributed renewables are the fastest route for remote areas — mini-grids and off-grid solar delivered 55% of the region's new connections in 2020–2022 — but the report stresses that sustained international finance is essential to close the gap.

interventions.title: Distributed renewables — mini-grids and solar home systems
interventions.evidence: The 2025 Tracking SDG7 report finds mini-grids and off-grid solar provided 55% of new electricity connections in sub-Saharan Africa in 2020–2022, making them the fastest route for remote and fragile areas.
interventions.actor: ESMAP, GEAPP and private off-grid solar companies

interventions.title: Mission 300 grid and off-grid electrification
interventions.evidence: The World Bank and African Development Bank's Mission 300 aims to connect 300 million people in sub-Saharan Africa by 2030 — roughly half via grid extension and half via off-grid solutions — backed by a USD 30bn World Bank commitment, with over 50 million people connected by mid-2026.
interventions.actor: World Bank, African Development Bank and national governments

interventions.title: Decentralised clean-cooking transition
interventions.evidence: Tracking SDG7 2025 highlights decentralised approaches — small LPG depots, household biogas, electric cooking via mini-grids and flexible financing — to cut the ~2.1 billion-person clean-cooking gap.
interventions.actor: National energy ministries, WHO and the Clean Cooking Alliance

interventions.title: Concessional finance and results-based grants for last-mile access
interventions.evidence: The five SDG7 custodian agencies stress that scaled international financial support and results-based grants are needed to reach the hardest-to-serve populations and address widening regional disparities.
interventions.actor: Multilateral development banks and donor governments
